era_context: The late Warring States period in China, with the Qin state rapidly consolidating power under Qin Shihuang's reign (begun -221). In the Mediterranean, the Second Punic War between Rome and Carthage is in its early stages (began -218). The Hellenistic world is fragmented among the successors of Alexander's empire. In India, the Mauryan Empire under Ashoka's successors is in decline.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records for this period)
Northern Europe beyond Greek and Roman contact zones
Pre-Columbian Americas (no written records; archaeological dating uncertain)

events.title: Qin consolidation of Zhao and Han states
events.description: Qin Shihuang's forces continue systematic conquest of rival states. By 220 BCE, Qin has already absorbed Chu, Zhao, and Han, leaving only Qi and Yan as major independent powers. These campaigns represent the final phase of the Warring States period and lay the groundwork for the first unified Chinese empire. The military innovations and administrative consolidation during this period establish templates for imperial governance.
key_figures: Qin Shihuang (King of Qin, later First Emperor)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian
Qin administrative records and bamboo slips

events.certainty_note: The Shiji provides detailed chronology, but was compiled ~300 years after events. Archaeological evidence from Qin-period administrative documents corroborates the general timeline of conquest, though precise dating of individual campaigns within 220 BCE is approximate.

events.title: Second Punic War: Roman operations in Spain and Sicily
events.description: The Second Punic War (218–201 BCE) is in its early phase. Roman forces under various commanders are engaged in Spain against Carthaginian forces and Iberian allies, while naval operations continue in the western Mediterranean. This war will ultimately determine hegemony in the western Mediterranean and marks Rome's emergence as a superpower. The conflict introduces Hannibal Barca as a major strategic threat.

sources.name: Livy, History of Rome (Ab urbe condita)

events.certainty_note: Multiple near-contemporary and later classical sources document the Second Punic War with substantial detail. The year 220 BCE falls within the well-attested early phase of the conflict.

events.title: Ptolemaic Egypt under Ptolemy III Euergetes
events.description: Ptolemy III Euergetes rules Egypt during a period of relative stability and cultural flourishing. The Ptolemaic dynasty maintains control of Egypt and significant eastern Mediterranean territories. This period sees continued Hellenization of Egyptian culture and the expansion of the Library of Alexandria. Ptolemy III's reign (246–222 BCE) is marked by military campaigns in Syria and Anatolia, though by 220 BCE the dynasty faces increasing pressure from the Seleucid Empire.
key_figures: Arsinoe II (Queen, wife of Ptolemy II; still venerated)
events.certainty_note: Ptolemaic chronology is well-established through papyrological evidence and Greek historical sources. Ptolemy III's reign dates are secure.

events.title: Seleucid Empire under Antiochus III (early reign)
events.description: Antiochus III (the Great) has recently ascended to the Seleucid throne (222 BCE) and is consolidating power. By 220 BCE, he is beginning campaigns to recover territories lost to Ptolemaic Egypt and to reassert Seleucid control over the eastern provinces. His reign will be marked by ambitious military campaigns and the temporary restoration of Seleucid power, though ultimately he will face defeat by Rome.
events.certainty_note: Antiochus III's accession is dated to 222 BCE by Polybius and later sources. The specific events of 220 BCE are less precisely documented, but his general consolidation and early campaigns are well-attested.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional dating of Qin Shihuang's reign and the conquest of rival states relies heavily on the Shiji, compiled centuries after the events. Modern scholars debate the precise chronology of individual campaigns within the 221–210 BCE period; some events attributed to specific years may be approximate or conflated. The Second Punic War's early campaigns are well-documented, but the exact sequence of events in 220 BCE is sometimes unclear in surviving sources, which were written decades or centuries later.
historiographic_note: For China, the primary source is the Shiji, a court-sponsored history written during the Han dynasty (~100 BCE), introducing potential bias toward Han-era perspectives and possible anachronism. Archaeological evidence from Qin-period administrative documents provides some corroboration but is fragmentary. For the Mediterranean, Polybius and Livy are the main sources, both written well after the events; Polybius had access to earlier sources and is generally considered more reliable, but both reflect Roman-centric perspectives. Egyptian sources are primarily administrative papyri, which are abundant but often lack narrative context. The Seleucid Empire is documented primarily through Greek and later sources; Seleucid-era primary texts are sparse.

graph_edges.note: Qin consolidation continues toward the unification completed in 221 BCE
Early phase of Second Punic War leads to Hannibal's crossing of the Alps
Ptolemaic and Seleucid powers are rivals in the eastern Mediterranean during this period
